sannirundheyya.meaning: should restrain; would block, hold back completely
sannirundheyya.gram: verb, optative 3rd person singular of sannirundhati (saṁ + ni + √rudh)

sannisādemi.meaning: I calm; I quieten, make settle down
sannisādemi.gram: verb, present tense 1st person singular (causative of sannisīdati, √sad)
breakdown: Root “to settle”; causative: to make settle, to calm.

sannivāreyya.meaning: should restrain; would check, keep in check
sannivāreyya.gram: verb, optative 3rd person singular of sannivāreti (causative; saṁ + ni + √var)
breakdown: Root “to obstruct”; causative: to ward off, restrain.

sannāmeyyā.gram: verb, optative 3rd person singular (causative of saṁ + √nam “to bend”)
breakdown: Root “to bend”; causative: to make bend, contract.

santhate.meaning: spread; covered (with a covering laid out)
breakdown: Root “to spread” with past participle suffix: spread, strewn.

santindriyo.meaning: with peaceful faculties; whose senses are calmed

santo.meaning: existing; being (present); (also) a good, peaceful person
santo.gram: present participle of atthi (√as), nominative singular

breakdown: Adjectival suffix: one who is content.
Root “to be satisfied” with abstract suffix: contentment.
santuṭṭhi.goenka: Santuṭṭhi, contentment, is praised in the suttas as a great wealth and named in the Maṅgalasutta among the highest welfares. For one walking on the path, contentment with little supports the simple life in which the mind can be trained without being scattered by craving for more.

santānakā.meaning: offspring; lineage; (figuratively) the entanglement of having children
breakdown: Root “to stretch”: santāna, a continuum, lineage.

santānaṁ of.meaning: of the good, of saints; (santānaṁ, genitive plural of santa)
santānaṁ of.note: uncertain: this key is a glossary parsing artifact — the English word “of” from the gloss “of saints” was attached to the headword santānaṁ; the form is the genitive plural of santa “good person, saint”.

santāressaṁ.meaning: I will make cross over; I shall carry across
santāressaṁ.gram: verb, future 1st person singular of santāreti (causative of santarati, √tar)
breakdown: Root “to cross”; causative: to make cross, to ferry over.

saparidaṇḍā.meaning: protected under penalty (a class of women whose violation is punished by the rod)
breakdown: Stick, rod; punishment. Together: guarded with the threat of punishment.

saparidaṇḍāti.gram: adjective, feminine plural + quotation particle ti
breakdown: Punishment all around; protection under penalty of the rod.
Possession, belongings; also a wife (pari + √gah “to take hold of”).

sapattabhārova.meaning: with only his wings as burden (like a bird carrying nothing but its feathers)

sapattasaṁvāsaññeva.meaning: just living together with what has been obtained; cohabiting as with a rival
sapattasaṁvāsaññeva.gram: masculine noun, compound (accusative singular) + emphatic yeva (sandhi)
breakdown: Just, precisely (the -ṁ + y- gives -ññ- in sandhi).

saraṇavaraggagāmino.meaning: going to the best and highest refuge

sareyyātha.meaning: you should remember; may you recollect
sareyyātha.gram: verb, optative 2nd person plural of sarati (√sar “to remember”)

sarūpato.meaning: with reference to its own form; according to its nature
sarūpato.gram: adjective with ablative suffix -to, from rūpa
breakdown: Ablative suffix: from, with reference to.

sasakkaṁ.gram: indeclinable adverb (accusative of sasakka used adverbially)

sassata.goenka: Sassata, “eternal”, names the eternalist position (sassatadiṭṭhi) — the wrong view that there is a permanent, everlasting self or world. The Buddha's teaching rejects both this eternalism and its opposite, annihilationism; through vipassanā the meditator experiences directly that all phenomena are anicca, arising and passing away, leaving no ground for a doctrine of permanence.

sassatādikaṁ diṭṭhiṁ.meaning: the view of eternalism and so forth; views beginning with the eternity-view
sassatādikaṁ diṭṭhiṁ.goenka: The lexicon notes that the Brahmajālasutta of the Dīghanikāya surveys the many kinds of micchā diṭṭhi (wrong view) prevailing at the Buddha's time: sassata diṭṭhi, which maintains the idea of permanence; antānantavāda, doctrines of the world's finitude and infinity; aparantakappika, speculation about the future; and others. All such speculative views are set aside by the direct experience of arising and passing away.

sasāmikā.meaning: having a husband; a married woman

satekicchāya.meaning: curable; pardonable (of an offence that can still be remedied)
breakdown: Cure, remedy (from tikicchati “treats, cures”, √kit). Together: having a cure, pardonable.

satha.meaning: (in the compound seyyāvasathapadīpeyya) bed, dwelling and material for lamps
breakdown: Material for lamps, lighting (mentioned in the source compound).
satha.note: uncertain: this headword is a fragment cut from a longer compound (seyyāvasatha-padīpeyya “bed + dwelling + lamp-materials”); satha alone is not an independent word here.

sati.meaning: awareness; mindfulness; the faculty of being aware in the present
sati.summary: A core meditation term: the awareness that, joined with sampajañña, makes satipaṭṭhāna.
sati.gram: feminine abstract noun, from sarati (√sar “to remember”)
breakdown: Abstract noun suffix: remembrance; in the meditative sense, present-moment awareness.
sati.goenka: Sati is awareness — the faculty of being aware in the present moment. The volumes introduce it as one of the seven saddhammas: saddhā (faith), sati (awareness), hirī (sense of shame), ottappa (moral sense of guilt), bāhusacca (ample learning), vīriya (diligence) and paññā (wisdom). It stands at the heart of the satipaṭṭhāna formula: the meditator “remains contemplating the body, ardent, with thorough understanding and aware (ātāpī sampajāno satimā), having put aside covetousness and grief towards the world”. The rendering “awareness” rather than “mindfulness” reflects this tradition's emphasis on direct, present-moment attention to the reality of mind and body; sati works together with sampajañña, the constant thorough understanding of impermanence.

satidovāriko.summary: An image from the suttas: sati guards the sense doors as a gatekeeper guards a city.
satidovāriko.goenka: The image of awareness as gatekeeper describes the noble disciple whose sati stands guard at the doors of the senses: whatever arises at eye, ear, nose, tongue, body or mind is met with awareness, so that unwholesome reactions do not slip in unnoticed.

satikaraṇīyameva.meaning: merely a matter of awareness; just something to remain aware of
satikaraṇīyameva.gram: adjective compound (nominative singular neuter) + emphatic eva
breakdown: To be done, a duty (potential participle of karoti).
Just, merely (enclitic; -ṁ + e- gives -m- in sandhi).

satinepakkena.meaning: with awareness and prudence; by mindful heedfulness

satipaṭṭhāna.meaning: establishment of awareness; the four foundations of mindfulness
satipaṭṭhāna.summary: The fourfold establishing of awareness on body, sensations, mind and mental contents.
breakdown: Setting up, establishing (pa + √ṭhā “to stand”); also analysed as upaṭṭhāna “attending upon”.
satipaṭṭhāna.goenka: Satipaṭṭhāna is the establishment of awareness in four fields, set out in the classic formula: the meditator dwells contemplating the body in the body, sensations in the sensations, mind in the mind and mental contents in the mental contents — in each case “ardent, with thorough understanding and aware, having put aside covetousness and grief towards the world” (ātāpī sampajāno satimā vineyya loke abhijjhādomanassaṁ). The volumes link the four satipaṭṭhānas to ānāpānassati: with the development and full engagement of ānāpānassati the four satipaṭṭhānas are fulfilled; the four satipaṭṭhānas when developed fulfil the seven factors of enlightenment; and the seven factors when developed fulfil realisation through wisdom and liberation.

sattakaya.meaning: the seven bodies (the seven permanent substances asserted in Pakudha Kaccāyana's doctrine)
sattakaya.note: uncertain: the lexicon cites the doctrine that these seven “do not move, do not change” (te na iñjanti, na vipariṇamanti) — a wrong view recorded in the suttas, not a teaching of the Buddha.

sattanikāye.meaning: in the class of beings; in whatever assemblage of beings

sattatiṁsa bodhipakkhiyodhamma.meaning: the thirty-seven factors of enlightenment; the thirty-seven qualities that side with awakening
breakdown: Belonging to the side or wing of, partaking in.
sattatiṁsa bodhipakkhiyodhamma.goenka: The thirty-seven bodhipakkhiyā dhammā are the factors, parts or limbs of enlightenment: the four satipaṭṭhānas, four right efforts, four bases of accomplishment, five faculties, five powers, seven factors of enlightenment and the Noble Eightfold Path. Together they map the whole training by which awareness and wisdom are developed to fulfilment.

satthu.meaning: of the Teacher; under the Teacher
satthu.gram: masculine agent noun (genitive/dative singular of satthā)
breakdown: Agent suffix: one who instructs, the Teacher.

satthā.meaning: the Teacher (an epithet of the Buddha)
satthā.gram: masculine agent noun (nominative singular of satthar)
breakdown: Agent suffix: the one who teaches.

saya.meaning: (in saya / sayagyi) teacher; gyi is a Burmese suffix denoting respect
saya.gram: noun (Burmese loan usage); as Pāli stem, an adjective from sayati “to lie”
saya.note: divergence: the lexicon glosses saya as the Burmese word for “teacher” (as in Sayagyi, the respectful title used for U Ba Khin); the Pāli stem saya- (from √sī “to lie down”) is a different word.

sayeyya.gram: verb, optative 3rd person singular of sayati (√sī)

breakdown: Root “to be born” with abstract suffix: being born, origin.

sañjāyati be.note: uncertain: this key is a glossary parsing artifact — the English word “be” from the gloss “be born, produced” was attached to the headword sañjāyati; see the entry sañjāyati.

saññatti.gram: feminine abstract noun, from saññāpeti (causative of saṁ + √ñā)
breakdown: Root “to know”; causative: to make known, convince.
Root “to restrain” with past participle suffix (saṁyata > saññata).
